events.name: Untreated tooth infection — danger window
events.description: General example only, not medical advice. An untreated dental abscess can spread and become life-threatening in as little as a few days if bacteria enter the bloodstream.

content.intro: An untreated dental abscess can spread beyond the tooth and become a medical emergency. This is a general example countdown, not personalized medical advice.
quickFacts: Facial swelling, fever, or difficulty swallowing/breathing are emergency warning signs.
Bacteria can spread to the jaw, sinuses, or bloodstream if left untreated.
Seek a dentist or emergency room immediately if symptoms worsen — don't wait for antibiotics alone to resolve it.
content.faqAnswer: There's no fixed timeline — severity varies by person and infection. In serious untreated cases, complications can escalate within days. If you suspect a spreading infection, seek care immediately rather than waiting.
content.disclaimer: Educational example only. Not a diagnosis or medical advice.

events.name: Alcohol leaving your system — typical example
events.description: General example based on average metabolism (~1 standard drink per hour). Actual time varies by body weight, amount consumed, food intake, and test type.

content.intro: The body metabolizes alcohol at a fairly steady rate, roughly one standard drink per hour, though this varies by person.
quickFacts: Body weight, sex, food intake, and hydration all affect metabolism speed.
Breath and blood tests detect alcohol for a shorter window than urine tests (EtG tests can detect it up to ~80 hours later).
There's no safe way to speed up alcohol metabolism — coffee, cold showers, and exercise don't work.
content.faqAnswer: For an average drinking session, most people clear the bulk of the alcohol from their system within 24 hours, though trace metabolites can be detected longer depending on the test.
content.disclaimer: Educational example only. Not personalized medical advice.

events.name: August
events.description: Live countdown to the first day of August.

content.intro: A simple live countdown to August 1st.
quickFacts: August is the 8th month of the year and has 31 days.
In the Northern Hemisphere, August marks late summer; in the Southern Hemisphere, late winter.
content.faqAnswer: This countdown ticks down to midnight (UTC) on August 1st.

events.name: Car seat expiration — typical example
events.description: Most car seats expire 6–10 years from their manufacture date, depending on the brand. Always check the expiration date molded into or printed on your specific seat.

content.intro: Car seats expire because the plastic degrades over time and safety standards evolve. This example uses a common 6-year window.
quickFacts: The expiration date is usually molded into the plastic shell or printed on a sticker.
Expiration windows range roughly 6–10 years depending on the manufacturer.
Never use a car seat past its expiration date, even if it looks undamaged.
content.faqAnswer: Check the specific expiration date on your seat — this countdown uses a common 6-year example, but your seat's actual date may be different.
content.disclaimer: Example only — always defer to the date printed on your specific car seat.

events.name: Sunburn healing — typical example
events.description: Mild to moderate sunburn typically heals within 3–7 days. Severe burns (blistering) can take longer and may need medical attention.

content.intro: Sunburn is skin inflammation from UV overexposure. Most mild cases heal on their own within about a week.
quickFacts: Cool compresses and moisturizer can ease discomfort during healing.
Peeling skin a few days in is normal as damaged cells shed.
Blistering, severe pain, fever, or chills warrant medical attention.
content.faqAnswer: Mild to moderate sunburn generally fades within 3–7 days. This example uses 7 days as a typical upper-range estimate.
content.disclaimer: Educational example only. Not personalized medical advice.

events.name: Antibiotics working for a UTI — typical example
events.description: Most people notice symptom improvement within 1–3 days of starting antibiotics for a UTI. Complete the full prescribed course even after symptoms improve.

content.intro: Antibiotics typically start reducing UTI symptoms within a couple of days, though the full course matters for clearing the infection completely.
quickFacts: Symptom relief often begins within 24–72 hours of the first dose.
A full course is usually 3–7 days depending on the antibiotic prescribed.
Stopping early, even if you feel better, can lead to recurrence or resistance.
content.faqAnswer: Most people feel noticeably better within 1–3 days. If symptoms haven't improved after 3 days, or worsen, contact your doctor.
content.disclaimer: Educational example only. Not personalized medical advice — follow your prescribing doctor's instructions.

events.name: Pregnancy test accuracy — typical example
events.description: Home pregnancy tests are most reliable from the first day of a missed period, roughly 14 days after ovulation/conception. Testing earlier increases the chance of a false negative.

content.intro: Home pregnancy tests detect hCG, a hormone that rises steadily after implantation. Accuracy improves the closer you test to your missed period.
quickFacts: Testing too early is the most common cause of a false negative.
First-morning urine tends to have the most concentrated hCG.
A positive result is rarely wrong; a negative result taken early is less reliable.
content.faqAnswer: For the most reliable result, wait until the first day of your missed period — about 14 days after ovulation.
content.disclaimer: Educational example only. Not personalized medical advice.
